acts.summary: Alberta's comprehensive environmental legislation governing environmental assessments, industrial approvals, air and water quality, substance releases, conservation and reclamation, potable water, hazardous substances and pesticides, waste management and recycling, and enforcement — protecting the environment while enabling responsible development.
acts.full_text:
Environmental Protection and Enhancement Act

Citation: RSA 2000, c E-12

The Environmental Protection and Enhancement Act (EPEA) is Alberta's principal environmental statute. It provides the legal framework for protecting the environment while enabling responsible economic development, covering everything from industrial approvals to waste management.

Structure (11 Parts)

  • Part 1 — Administration: Establishes the Minister's powers, delegation authority, appointment of environmental officers, and creation of advisory bodies. Includes provisions for public participation and access to environmental information.
  • Part 2 — Environmental Assessment Process, Approvals and Registrations: Sets out the environmental impact assessment process for major projects, the system of approvals and registrations for industrial activities, and the conditions that may be attached to approvals. Defines activities requiring approval in the Schedule of Activities.
  • Part 3 — Activities Requiring Notice: Defines activities that must be notified to the Department, including minor industrial operations and changes that do not require full approval but must be reported.
  • Part 4 — Environmental Appeals Board: Establishes the Environmental Appeals Board and defines its jurisdiction to hear appeals of ministerial and director decisions regarding approvals, enforcement orders, and other matters under the Act.
  • Part 5 — Release of Substances: Prohibits the release of substances that may cause a significant adverse effect on the environment. Establishes mandatory reporting of substance releases, spill response requirements, and the duty to remediate contamination.
  • Part 6 — Conservation and Reclamation: Requires operators to conserve and reclaim land disturbed by industrial activity. Establishes the reclamation certification process and security deposit requirements to ensure land is returned to equivalent capability.
  • Part 7 — Potable Water: Governs the quality and safety of drinking water. Sets standards for waterworks systems, water treatment, and the protection of drinking water sources.
  • Part 8 — Hazardous Substances and Pesticides: Regulates the manufacture, sale, storage, transport, use, and disposal of hazardous substances and pesticides. Requires registration and licensing for certain activities.
  • Part 9 — Waste Minimization, Recycling and Waste Management: Promotes waste reduction and recycling. Establishes requirements for waste management facilities (landfills, composting), extended producer responsibility programs, and beverage container recycling.
  • Part 10 — Enforcement: Provides enforcement tools including environmental protection orders, administrative penalties, stop orders, and the power to prosecute offences. Establishes penalty provisions for violations.
  • Part 11 — Miscellaneous Provisions: Contains transitional provisions, regulation-making authority, confidentiality provisions, and other general matters.

EPEA is supported by numerous regulations including the Activities Designation Regulation, Substance Release Regulation, Conservation and Reclamation Regulation, and Waste Control Regulation.

acts.summary: Governs the allocation, diversion, and use of water in Alberta, including licences, approvals, water management planning, flood and drought response, watershed protection, and remediation — ensuring sustainable stewardship of the province's water resources.
acts.full_text:
Water Act

Citation: RSA 2000, c W-3

The Water Act is Alberta's principal statute for the management and allocation of the province's water resources. It establishes the legal framework for water licensing, approvals, watershed planning, and enforcement.

Structure (14 Parts)

  • Part 1 — Consultation: Requires public consultation on significant water management decisions and establishes processes for stakeholder engagement through Watershed Planning and Advisory Councils (WPACs).
  • Part 2 — Planning and Environmental Assessment: Provides for the development of water management plans at the provincial and regional level, including the Framework for Water Management Planning and approved water management plans for specific basins.
  • Part 3 — Right to Divert and Priority of Rights: Establishes the system of priority-based water rights in Alberta. Defines who may divert water, the priority system (first in time, first in right), and the categories of water use (household, traditional agriculture, licensed).
  • Part 4 — Approvals, Licences, Preliminary Certificates, Registrations: Governs the issuance, amendment, suspension, and cancellation of water licences and approvals. Sets out conditions, security deposits, and the process for obtaining authorization to divert water or conduct activities affecting water bodies.
  • Part 5 — Changes in Ownership, Transfers: Establishes rules for the transfer of water licences and allocations between holders, including conditions and restrictions on licence transfers.
  • Part 6 — Water Management Works and Undertakings: Governs the construction, operation, and maintenance of water management infrastructure such as dams, canals, and drainage works.
  • Part 7 — Remedial Measures: Provides authority to order remediation when water resources are adversely affected, including restoration of watercourses and water bodies.
  • Part 8 — Notice: Establishes notice requirements for decisions, orders, and other administrative actions under the Act.
  • Part 9 — Appeals: Defines the right of appeal to the Environmental Appeals Board for decisions under the Act, including approvals, licences, and enforcement orders.
  • Part 10 — Inquiry and Enforcement Orders: Grants powers to conduct inquiries and issue enforcement orders, including water management orders and emergency flood or drought response orders.
  • Part 11 — Offences, Penalties and Related Matters: Establishes offences and penalties for violations of the Act, including unauthorized diversions, failure to comply with orders, and interference with water management works.
  • Part 12 — Civil Matters: Addresses civil liability arising from water use, including damage caused by flooding, unauthorized diversions, or failure to maintain water management works.
  • Part 13 — Miscellaneous Matters: Contains regulation-making authority, provisions for Crown immunity, and other general matters.
  • Part 14 — Transitional: Transitions rights and agreements from the previous Water Resources Act to the current Act.

The Water Act is supported by regulations including the Water (Ministerial) Regulation, Water (Offences and Penalties) Regulation, and various approved water management plans.

acts.summary: Governs the management, conservation, and protection of wildlife in Alberta, including Crown ownership of wildlife, hunting licences and permits, species at risk designations, possession and commerce in animals, and enforcement of wildlife regulations.
acts.full_text:
Wildlife Act

Citation: RSA 2000, c W-10

The Wildlife Act is Alberta's primary statute for the management and conservation of wildlife resources. It establishes Crown ownership of wildlife and provides the legal framework for hunting, species protection, and wildlife management.

Structure (9 Parts)

  • Part 1 — Administration: Establishes ministerial authority over wildlife management, provides for the appointment of wildlife officers, and sets out general administrative powers including wildlife surveys, management programs, and co-operative agreements with other jurisdictions.
  • Part 2 — Relationship of the Crown to Wildlife: Declares that all wildlife in Alberta is the property of the Crown. Establishes the Crown's right to manage wildlife populations and sets out the obligations of landowners in relation to wildlife on their property.
  • Part 3 — Licences, Permits and Authorizations: Governs the issuance of hunting licences, permits, and authorizations. Sets out eligibility requirements, allocation processes (including draws and quotas), and the conditions attached to various types of hunting and trapping authorizations.
  • Part 4 — Hunting and Related Activities: Regulates hunting practices, seasons, bag limits, methods, and prohibited activities. Includes provisions for the protection of wildlife during vulnerable periods and restrictions on hunting near occupied dwellings.
  • Part 5 — Possession of and Commerce in Animals: Controls the possession, sale, purchase, trade, and export of wildlife and wildlife products. Regulates taxidermy, wildlife exhibits, and the keeping of captive wildlife.
  • Part 6 — Enforcement: Establishes enforcement powers including search, seizure, inspection, and investigation. Provides for the forfeiture of equipment used in offences and the suspension of licences.
  • Part 7 — Evidence: Defines rules for the admissibility and presentation of evidence in legal proceedings under the Act, including presumptions and certificates.
  • Part 8 — Offences and Penalties: Lists offences and corresponding penalties for violations, including fines, imprisonment, and mandatory licence suspensions for serious offences such as poaching.
  • Part 9 — Regulations and Transitional Provisions: Authorizes the making of regulations covering species designations, hunting seasons, bag limits, and other matters. Includes transitional provisions from the previous Wildlife Act.

The Wildlife Act is supported by the Wildlife Regulation, which provides detailed rules for hunting seasons, bag limits, licensing, and species designations including the listing of species at risk.

acts.full_name: Emissions Management and Climate Resilience Act
acts.summary: Establishes Alberta's framework for managing greenhouse gas emissions from large industrial facilities, including the Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction (TIER) system, emissions reporting requirements, offset credits, and performance credits for reducing industrial carbon emissions.
acts.full_text:
Emissions Management and Climate Resilience Act

Citation: SA 2003, c C-16.7 (formerly the Climate Change and Emissions Management Act)

The Emissions Management and Climate Resilience Act (EMCRA) is Alberta's primary statute for regulating industrial greenhouse gas emissions. Originally enacted in 2003 as the Climate Change and Emissions Management Act, it was renamed and updated to reflect the province's evolving approach to emissions management and climate resilience.

Key Provisions

  • Emissions Reporting: Requires large industrial facilities (emitting 100,000 tonnes or more of CO₂ equivalent annually) to report their greenhouse gas emissions to the regulator. Establishes reporting methodologies, verification requirements, and timelines.
  • TIER Regulatory Framework: Enables the Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction (TIER) Regulation, which requires regulated facilities to meet facility-specific emissions benchmarks. Facilities that exceed their benchmark must comply by improving operations, using emissions offset credits, using emissions performance credits, or contributing to the TIER fund.
  • Emissions Offset Credits: Establishes the framework for generating, registering, and retiring offset credits from verified emission reduction projects across the province. Allows facilities to use offset credits toward their compliance obligations.
  • Emissions Performance Credits: Allows facilities that outperform their benchmark to generate emissions performance credits, which can be banked or sold to other regulated facilities.
  • TIER Fund: Establishes the fund into which compliance payments are directed. The fund supports technology and innovation to reduce greenhouse gas emissions in Alberta.
  • Climate Resilience: Provides authority for programs and initiatives related to climate adaptation and resilience, including support for communities and sectors adapting to climate change impacts.
  • Administration and Enforcement: Sets out administrative powers, inspection and audit authorities, penalties for non-compliance with reporting and reduction obligations, and the right of appeal.

EMCRA is supported by the Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction Regulation, the Carbon Competitiveness Incentive Regulation (predecessor), and the Standard for Greenhouse Gas Emission Offset Project Developers.

sections.title: Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reporting and Verification
sections.description: Requires large industrial emitters to report greenhouse gas emissions annually. Establishes methodologies, verification standards, and timelines for emissions reporting under the TIER framework.

services.name: Report greenhouse gas emissions under the regulatory framework

sections.title: Emissions Offset Credits and Performance Credits
sections.description: Establishes the framework for generating, registering, and trading emissions offset credits and performance credits. Allows regulated facilities to use credits toward compliance obligations.

services.name: Purchase or sell emissions offset credits and performance credits
